setup.title: Simulation Mission Setup
setup.missionDesc: You are assigned to conduct a radiation survey at a facility. Your main task is to measure the dose rate at designated locations (marked with special boxes in the simulation). Use knowledge from the briefing materials to keep your total dose received as low as possible.
setup.missionDesc2: Configure the simulation parameters below to begin.

game.title: Nuclear Radiation Simulation
game.controls: use W = ↗, A = ↙, Q = ↖, S = ↘ keys on keyboard to move the character and hover cursor over surrounding objects
game.mobileMessage: For the best simulation experience, please zoom out or use Desktop mode through your browser settings so the simulation is clearly visible.
game.desktopModeRequiredIntro: The simulation is temporarily locked to keep controls and layout accurate. Enable desktop mode in your browser, then press Re-check.
game.desktopStepChrome: Chrome on Android: tap the three-dot menu at the top right, then enable "Desktop site".
game.desktopStepSamsung: Samsung Internet: tap menu (☰), then enable "Desktop site".
game.desktopStepSafari: Safari on iOS: tap "aA" in the address bar, then choose "Request Desktop Website".
game.desktopStepAlternative: Alternative: zoom out until the simulation area is fully visible, then press Re-check.
game.desktopModeDiagnostic: Current status: viewport width {{viewportWidth}} px | zoom {{zoomScale}}x
game.desktopModeRecheckFailed: Desktop mode/zoom-out is not detected yet. Follow the steps above, then press Re-check again.
game.desktopModeCanvasLocked: Simulation area is locked until desktop mode/zoom-out is active.

safety.safe: SAFE: Dose rate below safety limit.
safety.warning: WARNING: Dose rate is quite high.
safety.danger: DANGER: Dose rate is very high.
safety.tooClose: DANGER: You are too close to the radiation source. Move away immediately!

tooltips.containerDesc: Storage for radiation sources. This container can shield radiation from reaching the environment or human body. This is because it is made of lead, which has high density. This container can store radiation sources both in laboratory or industrial scale.
tooltips.shieldingDesc: Radiation shield to block radiation emission
tooltips.effectivenessDesc: Reduces radiation based on Beer-Lambert law

results.title: Simulation Results
results.posttestRequired: The posttest is optional. You can fill it now or continue to the next step.
results.posttestUnlocked: Thank you, the posttest has been opened. Please continue with whichever next step you prefer.

veryLow.description: The dose you received is very low, equivalent to or even lower than the dose received from eating one banana (which contains Potassium-40).
veryLow.effects: No detectable health effects. Far below safety limits.

low.description: Equivalent to the dose from several intercontinental flights or several dental X-rays.
low.effects: Health risks at this level are considered negligible and statistically undetectable.

moderate.description: You have received a dose equivalent to several CT scan procedures. This is still within the range of annual background radiation variation in different parts of the world.
moderate.effects: Stochastic risk (e.g., cancer) slightly increases, but is very difficult to distinguish from general population risk. The annual dose limit for the general public is 1000 µSv (1 mSv).

high.description: This dose is above the annual limit for the general public. Radiation workers have higher limits (average 20,000 µSv per year).
high.effects: Statistically measurable increase in cancer risk if such exposure occurs repeatedly. No deterministic effects (nausea, skin redness) will appear.

veryHigh.description: You have received a significant dose. This level requires monitoring and further evaluation in a real scenario.
veryHigh.effects: Long-term (stochastic) risk increases significantly. The higher the dose, the greater the risk. Approaching levels where early deterministic effects (such as blood changes) may begin to be detected in sensitive individuals.

sourceDesc.cs-137: A commonly used radioactive isotope in medical and industrial applications. Emits high-energy gamma rays.
sourceDesc.co-60: A high-activity radioactive isotope, widely used in cancer therapy and industrial sterilization.
sourceDesc.na-22: An artificially produced isotope, used in research and detector calibration.
sourceDesc.am-241: An alpha-emitting isotope with low-energy gamma lines, commonly used in smoke detectors and calibration sources.
sourceDesc.u-235: A naturally occurring fissile isotope that is central to the nuclear fuel cycle, with a characteristic 185.7 keV gamma line.
sourceDesc.th-232: A very long-lived fertile nuclide with weak gamma emission, relevant for natural-source shielding simulations.
sourceDesc.pu-239: A man-made fissile isotope used in MOX fuel and safeguards analysis, with relatively low gamma emission.
sourceDesc.i-131: A short-lived fission product widely used in nuclear medicine and post-accident radiological monitoring.
sourceDesc.default: A radioactive isotope for various scientific and industrial applications.

materialDesc.lead: The most effective material for gamma radiation shielding due to its high density and atomic number.
materialDesc.concrete: An economical and practical construction material for large-scale radiation shielding.
materialDesc.glass: Special lead-containing glass that provides protection while maintaining transparency.
materialDesc.steel: High-density alloy steel, commonly used as structural shielding in nuclear facilities.
materialDesc.default: High-quality radiation shielding material for optimal protection against gamma radiation exposure.

edu.title: Education
edu.whatIsDecayDesc: Radioactive decay is the process by which an unstable atomic nucleus loses energy by emitting radiation. In this simulation, a piece of radioactive rock emits radiation particles and gradually dims as its atoms reach a stable state.
edu.halfLifeDesc: Half-life is the time required for half of the radioactive atoms in the rock to decay. The more frequently the rock emits radiation, the faster it dims.
edu.decayLawDesc: Decay is statistical; although the emission looks random, the decline curve is highly predictable mathematically, as shown in the graph on the right.

radiationTypes.title: Radiation Penetration
radiationTypes.legendGamma: Pure energy wave. High penetrating power.
radiationTypes.infoNone: Air: Radiation travels freely. Alpha has the lowest penetrating power in air, while Beta and Gamma can travel long distances.
radiationTypes.infoPaper: Paper: A single sheet of paper is thick enough to stop all Alpha (α) particles due to their large mass and charge.
radiationTypes.infoAlum: Aluminum Plate: Can absorb and stop Beta (β) particles, which are small like electrons. Gamma (γ) rays still pass through.
radiationTypes.infoLead: Lead Block (Pb): A material with very high atomic density. Highly effective at absorbing and weakening Gamma (γ) rays.
radiationTypes.theoryContent1: Ionizing radiation is mainly divided into three types of particles/waves: Alpha (α), Beta (β), and Gamma (γ). Each type has different mass, charge, and energy levels, which directly affect its ability to penetrate shielding materials.
radiationTypes.theoryContent2: Alpha particles have the largest mass (2 protons + 2 neutrons) and a strong positive charge, making them interact heavily with matter and can be stopped by just a sheet of paper. Beta particles are high-speed electrons that penetrate paper but are stopped by metal plates like Aluminum. Meanwhile, Gamma rays are pure electromagnetic waves with no mass or charge, giving them extreme penetrating power that can only be effectively weakened by high-density materials like Lead (Pb) blocks.

rutherford.title: Rutherford Scattering
rutherford.infoThomson: J.J. Thomson's Plum Pudding Model: The atom is an evenly spread positive sphere. Since the charge is not concentrated, heavy Alpha particles pass straight through without significant deflection.
rutherford.infoRutherford: Rutherford Model: The atom is mostly empty space with a dense Nucleus at the center. Particles coming very close to the nucleus are sharply deflected due to intense Coulomb repulsion.
rutherford.theoryContent1: In 1909, physicist Ernest Rutherford designed an experiment where alpha particles were fired at a very thin sheet of gold foil. According to J.J. Thomson's (Plum Pudding) atomic model believed at the time, the alpha particles should have passed straight through because the atom was considered a low-density cloud of positive charge.
rutherford.theoryContent2: However, the experiment shocked the scientific world: although most particles passed straight through (proving atoms are mostly empty space), a tiny fraction of alpha particles were deflected at sharp angles or even bounced straight back! This proved the existence of the 'Atomic Nucleus' — a minuscule structure at the center of the atom that is incredibly dense and strongly positively charged, repelling alpha particles via intense Coulomb force.
